steps.title: Map every dollar leaving your account — without judgment
steps.description: Pull the last 2 months of bank and card statements. Write down every expense category and its total. The goal isn't to feel bad — it's to see clearly. You can't change what you haven't named.
steps.impact: Creates a complete picture of where the $700 gap is coming from. Most people find $200-400 in spending they genuinely forgot about or didn't realize was recurring.

steps.title: Find and cut $700/month to stop the bleeding
steps.description: Using your expense map, identify cuts that bring spending to $3,800 or below. Start with subscriptions, dining, and variable spending — these move fastest. This isn't punishment; it's stopping the spiral before it gets harder to reverse.
steps.impact: Breaking even stops $8,400/year in new debt accumulation. Every month you delay, the hole gets deeper and harder to climb out of.

steps.title: Build a $500 crisis buffer — your first safety net
steps.description: Once spending is at or below income, redirect any freed-up cash toward a small emergency fund. Even $500 changes your relationship with unexpected expenses — it means the next surprise doesn't automatically become more debt.
steps.impact: Breaks the cycle where emergencies force more credit card use. A small buffer is psychologically powerful — it creates breathing room that makes every other step easier.

steps.title: Explore ways to bring in more income
steps.description: Cutting alone may feel suffocating. Look honestly at whether there's room to earn more — extra hours, a skill you can offer, selling things you no longer need. Even $200-300/month extra changes the math significantly.
steps.impact: An extra $300/month applied to the card reduces payoff time and interest meaningfully. Income growth also reduces the emotional weight of restriction.

steps.title: Make a structured plan to pay down the credit card
steps.description: Once you're breaking even, put every extra dollar toward the $10,000 balance. Pay more than the $250 minimum whenever possible. Even an extra $100/month saves hundreds in interest and shortens the timeline noticeably.
steps.impact: At 21% APR, the card costs roughly $175/month in interest alone. Reducing the balance is one of the highest-return moves available to you right now.

steps.title: Check in, reflect, and adjust with compassion
steps.description: Review what changed over 90 days. Where did you succeed? Where did old patterns resurface? This isn't about perfection — it's about understanding yourself better. Adjust the plan based on what you've learned, not what you think you 'should' have done.
steps.impact: Sustainable financial change comes from self-awareness, not willpower alone. A 90-day review builds the habit of honest reflection that keeps progress going long-term.

rawResponseBody.overallMessage: You're in a difficult place, and you already know it — that awareness matters more than it might feel right now. The $700 monthly gap is the one thing that has to change first. Everything else — the debt, the savings, the future — becomes possible once the spiral stops. This plan moves gently but deliberately. You don't have to fix everything at once.

steps.title: Build a $500 fire extinguisher fund — immediately
steps.description: Before anything else, get $500 into a separate high-yield savings account. Cut one subscription, sell something, pick up one extra shift. $250 is not a cushion — it's a joke. Fix that first.
steps.impact: Stops the cycle where every small emergency gets charged to the CC, undoing all your debt progress before it even starts.

steps.title: Nuke the minimum-only habit — start avalanche payments
steps.description: You're paying $250/mo and $175 of it is just interest. That's a subscription fee to stay broke. Find $200-300 extra and throw it at the CC balance on top of the minimum. Every dollar over minimum kills future interest.
steps.impact: Dropping an extra $250/mo cuts payoff time from 6+ years to under 3 and saves roughly $3,000+ in interest charges.

steps.title: Forensic audit your $3,200 in expenses
steps.description: Your expense confidence is LOW — meaning even you don't know where the money goes. Pull 3 months of statements. Categorize everything. Find the leaks. FL no-income-tax is a gift — stop wasting it on mystery spending.
steps.impact: Most people find $150-300/mo in forgotten or redundant spending. That money goes straight to the CC — no lifestyle change required.

steps.title: Lock in a monthly budget and automate the attack
steps.description: Set fixed auto-transfers on payday: CC extra payment first, then savings, then you live on what's left. Remove willpower from the equation entirely. If you have to decide every month, you'll lose every month.
steps.impact: Automation turns a chaotic financial life into a system. Consistent $450-500/mo toward CC gets you debt-free in roughly 26 months.

steps.title: Explore one income boost — even a small one
steps.description: Your income is the lever here. Even $300-400/mo from freelance work, overtime, or selling stuff goes 100% to the CC. You don't need a second career — just a temporary income spike to accelerate the payoff.
steps.impact: An extra $350/mo on top of your current attack payment could shave another 8-10 months off the payoff timeline.

steps.title: Check the scoreboard and build toward a real emergency fund
steps.description: By month 3, your CC balance should be noticeably lower. Review progress, adjust payments if income changed, and start planning to grow your emergency fund to 1 full month of expenses once the CC is under $7k.
steps.impact: Hitting $3,200 in liquid savings transforms your risk profile from 'one bad week from disaster' to actually stable. That's the real goal.

rawResponseBody.overallMessage: You've got real income, no state tax drag, and one clear villain: a $10k CC charging you $175/mo just to exist. The first 30 days are about stopping the bleeding — tiny savings buffer, then go hard on that balance. After that it's automation and discipline. You're not broke, you're just paying a stupidity tax. Let's cancel that subscription.

steps.title: Build a $1,000 starter emergency fund — before anything else
steps.description: Park $1,000 in a separate high-yield savings account and don't touch it. This is your firewall. Without it, every unexpected expense lands back on the 24% card and undoes your progress.
steps.impact: Breaks the 'emergency → more CC debt' cycle. One car repair or medical bill won't reset months of hard work.

steps.title: Lock in the $400 extra CC payment — automate it on payday
steps.description: Set up an automatic payment of $616/mo total ($216 minimum + $400 extra) the day after your paycheck hits. Automate it so it's not a decision you have to make every month.
steps.impact: Card paid off in ~14 months instead of 3+ years. Saves roughly $1,800–$2,000 in interest charges.

steps.title: Audit every recurring charge and plug the leaks
steps.description: Pull 3 months of statements. Cancel forgotten subscriptions. Set a hard weekly cap on discretionary spending — dining, entertainment, impulse buys. Your expenses are estimated low-confidence, so find out what's actually happening.
steps.impact: Even finding $100–150/mo in leaks accelerates your payoff timeline and gives you breathing room without cutting anything you actually care about.

steps.title: Tackle the rent problem — explore one real option
steps.description: At 37.5% of take-home, rent is above the safe threshold. Pick one lever: a roommate, a side income stream, or a job search targeting a $5,500+/mo take-home. You don't need all three — just one.
steps.impact: Dropping rent to 30% of income or raising income by $700/mo frees up cash to accelerate debt payoff AND start building real savings simultaneously.

steps.title: Redirect CC payments into a 3-month emergency fund
steps.description: Once the card balance is under $3,000, start splitting the $400 extra — half to the card, half to savings. When the card is gone, redirect the full $616/mo to savings until you hit 3 months of expenses (~$11,700).
steps.impact: Transforms your financial profile from 'one bad month from freefall' to genuinely stable. This is the step that changes how money feels day-to-day.

steps.title: Check the scoreboard and lock in your next 90 days
steps.description: Sit down with your numbers. Card balance down? Emergency fund growing? If yes, automate everything and set a new target. If not, find the one thing that slipped and fix just that.
steps.impact: Consistent monthly check-ins beat willpower every time. People who review their finances monthly save 2–3x more than those who don't.

rawResponseBody.overallMessage: You've got solid income and you know your numbers — that's more than most people start with. The $7,200 card is the villain here, and $0 in savings is what keeps it winning. First 30 days: build the buffer so life stops resetting you. Then hammer the debt. You're not far from a completely different financial situation — 14 months is nothing.

steps.title: Calculate your exact rent-to-income ratio
steps.description: Divide your monthly rent by your take-home pay. If it's above 35%, you have a confirmed housing cost problem — not a spending problem. Knowing the real number is the first act of self-care here.
steps.impact: Replaces vague financial anxiety with a clear, specific number you can actually work with. Clarity is the foundation of every next step.

steps.title: Map every dollar: income, rent, and what's left
steps.description: Write out your full monthly picture — income in, rent out, then every other expense. No judgment, just facts. This shows you exactly where the pressure is coming from and where small changes are possible.
steps.impact: Turns a feeling of being overwhelmed into a concrete budget you can adjust. Most people find at least $100–200 in overlooked spending during this step.

steps.title: Explore one concrete option to reduce housing cost
steps.description: If rent is above 35% of take-home, brainstorm one realistic lever: a roommate, a different neighborhood, negotiating your renewal, or a longer-term relocation plan. You don't have to act yet — just identify the option.
steps.impact: Even a $200–400/month reduction in housing cost would meaningfully improve your savings rate and reduce financial stress over time.

steps.title: Build your emergency fund to 1 full month of expenses
steps.description: You currently have about 10 days of expenses saved. Aim to reach one full month (~$4,200) by setting aside a fixed amount each payday. Automate it so it happens before you can spend it.
steps.impact: One month of savings transforms a single unexpected expense from a crisis into an inconvenience. It's the single biggest stress-reducer at your stage.

steps.title: Look for small income boosts that fit your life
steps.description: With housing costs this high, earning a little more can matter as much as spending less. Think about skills you already have — freelance work, consulting, or picking up extra hours — even $200–300/month helps.
steps.impact: An extra $200–300/month directed to savings could get you to a 3-month emergency fund in under a year without changing your lifestyle.

steps.title: Review your progress and set a 6-month savings goal
steps.description: Look back at what changed. Did the budget reveal anything? Did you act on a housing option? Use what you've learned to set a realistic 6-month savings target and automate transfers to reach it.
steps.impact: Turning a 90-day effort into a 6-month habit is where real financial stability begins. You'll end this period with more savings, more clarity, and less stress.

rawResponseBody.overallMessage: Living in New York is genuinely hard, and your stress about housing costs is completely valid. You're not bad with money — you're navigating one of the most expensive places in the world. This plan starts with clarity, builds a cushion, and gently explores ways to ease the pressure. One step at a time, you've got this.

steps.title: Park the $10k in a high-yield savings account — now
steps.description: Before you touch a single investment, move that $10k into a high-yield savings account. You have $250 in liquid savings. One car repair, one ER visit, one anything ends you. This is not optional.
steps.impact: Instantly transforms your emergency fund from 0.09 months to nearly 4 months of expenses. You go from 'one bad day from disaster' to actually stable.

steps.title: Lock in a monthly savings habit before you invest a dime
steps.description: Set up an automatic transfer of at least $300/month into that savings account on payday. You're already saving $700/mo — this just makes it intentional and untouchable.
steps.impact: Builds a real financial floor. After 3 months you'll have a fully funded emergency fund AND leftover capital to actually invest without gambling your safety net.

steps.title: Audit where your $2,800/month is actually going
steps.description: Pull 3 months of bank statements. Categorize every dollar. With low-confidence income and expense numbers, you don't actually know your real picture yet — and that's a problem.
steps.impact: Reveals hidden leaks and confirms whether your $700/mo savings is real or just math on paper. Knowing your real numbers is the foundation of every good financial decision.

steps.title: Learn what you're actually investing in before investing
steps.description: Spend 30 minutes a week reading about diversified, low-cost index funds. Understand the difference between speculation and investing. No tickers, no crypto picks — just the fundamentals.
steps.impact: Investors who understand what they own make better decisions and panic-sell less. This 30-day education phase will make your first real investment 10x smarter.

steps.title: Start investing — the right way, with money you can afford
steps.description: Once your emergency fund is solid and your budget is confirmed, begin investing a fixed monthly amount (not a lump sum) into a diversified, low-cost index fund. Start small. Stay consistent.
steps.impact: You finally become an investor — but one with a safety net, a real budget, and a strategy. That's the version that actually builds wealth instead of just vibes.

steps.title: Check the scoreboard and adjust
steps.description: Review your savings rate, emergency fund balance, and investment contributions. Are the numbers real now? Adjust your monthly auto-transfers based on what actually happened vs. what you planned.
steps.impact: Turns a 90-day sprint into a permanent system. One monthly check-in beats 30 days of anxiety every time.

rawResponseBody.overallMessage: You have $10k and zero safety net — that's not an investment opportunity, that's a trap waiting to spring. The move isn't sexy: park the money, build the floor, then invest what you can actually afford to invest. Do it in that order and in 90 days you'll be a real investor. Skip the order and you're just gambling with your rent money.

steps.title: Track every dollar for 30 days — no exceptions
steps.description: Your expenses are listed at $7,500/mo but confidence is low. With $15k coming in and $50k in CC debt, money is leaking somewhere. Use any method — spreadsheet, notes app, whatever — to log every transaction for 30 days.
steps.impact: You can't fix a spending problem you can't see. This one step will reveal exactly where the surplus is going and give you a real number to work with.

steps.title: Build a $5,000 emergency buffer before going hard on debt
steps.description: You have ~$6k liquid but only 0.8 months of coverage. Top this up to a clean $5,000 buffer in a high-yield savings account and leave it alone. This is your firewall against going deeper into the cards.
steps.impact: Stops the cycle of paying down cards then charging them back up when life happens. One unexpected expense won't derail the whole plan.

steps.title: Launch the avalanche: $3,500/mo extra toward Card 1
steps.description: All three cards are at 21%, so pick the highest balance and go hard. On top of the $340 minimum, throw $3,500/mo at Card 1. Set up an automatic transfer on payday — don't let it sit in checking.
steps.impact: Card 1 gone in roughly 5 months. You'll save ~$1,800 in interest on that card alone and free up $340/mo to redirect to Card 2.

steps.title: Find and cut the lifestyle leaks funding this debt
steps.description: Pull 3 months of statements. Look for dining, subscriptions, impulse purchases, and anything recurring you forgot about. Set a hard monthly cap on discretionary spending and treat it like a bill.
steps.impact: High earners with CC debt almost always have $500-1,500/mo in untracked lifestyle spend. Cutting even half of that accelerates your payoff timeline by months.

steps.title: Roll the avalanche to Card 2 the moment Card 1 is gone
steps.description: The second Card 1 hits zero, redirect that entire payment — minimum plus extra — straight to Card 2. Don't let the freed-up cash get absorbed into lifestyle. Automate it immediately.
steps.impact: Card 2 cleared in roughly 4 months after Card 1. Momentum compounds fast — by Month 3 you'll have eliminated $34k of debt and ~$600/mo in minimums.

steps.title: Review progress, lock in a savings rate, and plan what comes next
steps.description: By now you should have 1-2 cards gone and real momentum. Revisit your actual spending data, confirm your payoff timeline for Card 3, and decide what you're doing with the freed-up cash — retirement, investing, or a real emergency fund.
steps.impact: At this pace, you're debt-free in 12-15 months. After that, $4,000+/mo that was going to interest and minimums becomes wealth-building fuel.

rawResponseBody.overallMessage: You've got the income to fix this in under 18 months — most people would kill for that runway. The hard truth is this isn't a math problem, it's a habits problem. The plan is simple: find the leaks, build a small buffer, then throw everything at the cards in order. Stay the course and you'll be debt-free before next summer.

steps.title: Open a high-yield savings account and set up auto-transfer
steps.description: Open a high-yield savings account (separate from your checking) and auto-transfer $50-75 on payday. Out of sight, out of mind. This single move builds the habit that compounds for decades.
steps.impact: By Month 3 you'll have $150-225 stacked on top of your current $250 — nearly doubling your emergency buffer on autopilot.

steps.title: Map every dollar of your $700 in monthly expenses
steps.description: Write down every recurring expense — subscriptions, food, transport, everything. You're working with a tight income, so knowing exactly where $700 goes is your superpower. Find even $25 hiding in there.
steps.impact: Identifying even one or two leaks frees up cash to accelerate your emergency fund without earning a single dollar more.

steps.title: Set a $500 emergency fund target as your first milestone
steps.description: Right now you're one busted laptop or urgent prescription away from needing a credit card. Hit $500 saved first — it's a real psychological and financial turning point. That's your immediate goal.
steps.impact: Crossing $500 means most common student emergencies are covered in cash, not debt. Keeps your zero-debt streak alive.

steps.title: Explore one income boost — even $100-200/month more changes everything
steps.description: At your income level, earning more moves the needle faster than cutting. Think campus jobs, tutoring, freelance gigs, or selling stuff you don't use. Even one extra shift a week adds up fast.
steps.impact: An extra $150/month means your savings rate jumps from 22% to nearly 35% — putting you in genuinely strong financial shape for a student.

steps.title: Push your emergency fund target to 1 full month of expenses
steps.description: Once you hit $500, keep the momentum going to $700 — one full month of expenses. This is the real safety net. Redirect any extra income or spending wins straight here.
steps.impact: One month of expenses saved means a gap in work hours, a medical bill, or a travel emergency doesn't derail your finances or force you into debt.

steps.title: Review, celebrate, and set your next 90-day target
steps.description: Look back at what you built. Did the auto-transfer hold? Did income go up? Adjust the savings amount, set a new milestone, and lock in the habits before life gets more complex post-graduation.
steps.impact: The habits you lock in now — before real income, before lifestyle inflation — are worth more than any single financial decision you'll make in your 20s.

rawResponseBody.overallMessage: Zero debt as a student is genuinely elite — most people your age are already in the hole. Your only real job right now is to protect that advantage and build the savings habit before life gets expensive. 90 days from now you could have a real emergency fund, a locked-in savings habit, and maybe a little more income. Let's build the foundation that future-you will be hyped about.

steps.title: Calculate your 'dead money' — cash sitting above your emergency fund
steps.description: You have $80k liquid and 11+ months of expenses covered. Anything above 6 months (~$42k) is just rotting in a low-yield account. Identify that number. That's your deployment target.
steps.impact: Knowing the exact amount of underdeployed cash turns a vague problem into a concrete number you can actually act on.

steps.title: Move excess cash into a high-yield savings account immediately
steps.description: Park the surplus (roughly $38k+) in a high-yield savings account earning 4-5% APY while you decide on longer-term moves. Takes 10 minutes. Do it now.
steps.impact: At 4.5% APY on $38k, that's ~$1,700/year in interest you're currently leaving on the table. Every month you wait is money lost.

steps.title: Max out your HSA if you're eligible — it's the triple tax cheat code
steps.description: If you have a high-deductible health plan, an HSA is the only account that's pre-tax in, tax-free growth, and tax-free out. Contribute the annual max before touching anything else.
steps.impact: Triple tax advantage beats a Roth IRA on pure math. If eligible and not using it, you're overpaying taxes for no reason.

steps.title: Open a taxable brokerage and start investing the surplus monthly
steps.description: Set up a taxable brokerage account and automate a monthly transfer of your surplus savings into a diversified, low-cost index fund strategy. No stock picking. No timing the market. Just consistent, automated investing.
steps.impact: Historically, broad market index investing has outpaced inflation by 5-7% annually. Your cash is currently losing ~2-3% in real value every year it sits idle.

steps.title: Check if you can contribute to a Roth IRA — income limits apply
steps.description: At $12k/month income, you may be near or above the Roth IRA income phase-out. Verify your eligibility. If you're over the limit, look into a backdoor Roth conversion — it's legal and worth it.
steps.impact: Tax-free growth on up to $7,000/year compounded over decades is a significant long-term wealth multiplier.

steps.title: Do a full wealth audit — are your systems actually optimized?
steps.description: Review: Is your 401k allocation actually diversified or just sitting in a default fund? Are you tracking net worth monthly? Do you have a will or basic estate documents? Fix the gaps.
steps.impact: At your level, the biggest risks aren't income or debt — they're complacency and misallocation. A quarterly audit keeps your wealth working as hard as you do.

rawResponseBody.overallMessage: You're not broke. You're not even close. But 'thriving' and 'optimized' are two different things — and right now you've got tens of thousands of dollars slowly losing value to inflation while you sleep. The next 90 days are about putting that money to work so your wealth compounds instead of stagnates. No debt to kill, no emergencies to survive. Just pure offense.
